does: This paper proposes CIDER, a plug-and-play detection system that identifies jailbreak attacks in multimodal large language models by leveraging cross-modality information consistency. The method checks whether text and image inputs are aligned or show signs of adversarial manipulation, allowing it to flag potentially harmful prompts without modifying the target model's internal structure. By analyzing the relationship between visual and textual modalities, CIDER exploits the observation that jailbreak attempts often introduce misalignment between image and text components—malicious users craft inputs where images contradict or manipulate the safety intent of textual prompts. The approach operates at inference time with minimal computational overhead and achieves detection of jailbreaking attempts across multiple attack scenarios.
connects: This work builds on recent research documenting the vulnerability of multimodal models to adversarial attacks, extending earlier safety alignment work from pure language models like those in the constitutional AI and reinforcement learning from human feedback traditions. It responds to the practical limitation of existing defense mechanisms that require costly architectural modifications or intensive computational resources, problems identified in concurrent research on efficient safety interventions. The paper connects to broader work on multimodal representation learning and cross-modal alignment, which has shown that vision-language models exploit correlations between modalities. It also relates to adversarial example detection literature, adapting those insights to the multimodal setting where attackers now have additional degrees of freedom through vision inputs.
enables: This work enables more practical deployment of safety checks for multimodal models in resource-constrained settings by providing a lightweight, externally-applied defense layer. It points toward future research on understanding how adversarial perturbations manifest across modalities and whether similar consistency-checking approaches could detect other forms of model misuse. The plug-and-play nature suggests follow-up work on combining CIDER with other orthogonal safety techniques and developing adaptive attacks that maintain cross-modal alignment while still achieving jailbreak goals. More broadly, it opens investigation into whether multimodal redundancy can systematically enhance rather than degrade model safety, potentially informing the design of more robust future multimodal systems.
